model_narrative: We plan shipments of a product from a set of canning plants to a set of markets. For each plant-to-market arc we decide how many cases to ship and whether to use that arc at all. Shipping incurs a per-case transport cost that varies by arc, and using an arc at all incurs a one-time fixed charge specific to that arc. The objective is to minimize the total transportation cost, combining the per-case shipping costs and the fixed charges of the arcs that are used.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the set of canning plants that can ship product, each a supply origin

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the set of markets that receive product, each a demand destination

params.name: a
params.doc: the production capacity of each plant, in cases; the most that plant can ship out across all markets

params.name: b
params.doc: the demand at each market, in cases; the amount that market must receive

params.name: c
params.doc: the per-case transport cost on the arc from a plant to a market, in thousands of dollars per case

params.name: fixcost
params.doc: the fixed charge incurred on a plant-to-market arc whenever that arc is used at all, in thousands of dollars; charged once regardless of volume

params.name: minshipping
params.doc: the minimum number of cases that must flow on any arc that is used, in cases; a single scalar applying to every arc

params.name: bigM
params.doc: a single large constant, set to the largest plant capacity, used to cap flow on an arc to zero when that arc is not used; a scalar

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the shipment quantity sent on each plant-to-market arc, in cases

vars.name: use
vars.doc: arc-use indicator; equals 1 if the plant-to-market arc carries any shipment and 0 otherwise

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total transportation cost over all arcs, in thousands of dollars

constraints.name: supply
constraints.intent: Each plant can ship out no more than its production capacity. For every plant, the total amount shipped from it across all markets must not exceed that plant's capacity.

constraints.name: demand
constraints.intent: Every market must receive at least the amount it demands. For each market, the total amount shipped into it from all plants must be at least its demand.

constraints.name: minship
constraints.intent: If an arc is used to ship product, the amount sent on it must meet a minimum shipping level. For each plant-to-market arc, the amount shipped must be at least the minimum shipping quantity whenever that arc is marked as used, and may be nothing when the arc is not used.

constraints.name: maxship
constraints.intent: No product may flow on an arc unless that arc is marked as used. For each plant-to-market arc, the amount shipped must be zero when the arc is not used, and otherwise may go up to a large cap that does not otherwise restrict the flow.

constraints.name: cost
constraints.intent: The total transportation cost gathers both the per-case shipping costs and the one-time fixed charges. Across every plant-to-market arc, value the cases shipped at that arc's per-case transport cost and add the arc's fixed charge whenever the arc is used. Set the total cost equal to the sum of these amounts over all arcs.

query: We're looking at a single-sourcing risk on the New York lane — right now San Diego carries that whole market. There's a proposal on the table to keep San Diego's share of everything moving into New York to no more than half of the combined New York inflow from both plants, so Seattle has to pick up the rest. What does the optimizer return for total freight cost under that split?

query: Engineering is pitching a plan to plant a firmer Seattle footprint on the two markets it barely touches today — the New York corridor and the Topeka plains lane. We're looking at requiring Seattle's combined outbound into those two destinations to add up to at least 200 cases a cycle. Plug that in and tell us where the total transportation bill lands.

query: There's a proposal to make San Diego the anchor supplier for the two big eastern accounts, New York and Chicago, so we don't have to lean on Seattle for those. We're looking at a scenario where San Diego's deliveries into New York and Chicago together come to at least 500 cases. What total cost does the optimizer come back with if we lock that in?

query: The board is weighing a two-part operating change for next quarter: run the Seattle plant at its full 350-case throughput so it isn't sitting idle, and at the same time pull San Diego back off the Topeka lane to no more than 150 cases so we stop over-relying on that one cheap corridor. We want to evaluate both together as a package — plug the pair in and walk us through the resulting freight bill.
expected_pyomo:
model.sea_full = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['seattle', j] for j in model.j) >= 350)
model.cap_sdtop = Constraint(expr=model.x['san-diego', 'topeka'] <= 150)

query: Topeka is being served out of a single plant — only the San Diego arc into it shows as active, and the Seattle-to-Topeka lane is dark. For continuity if one source hiccups, shouldn't both plants have a live arc standing open into Topeka rather than betting the whole market on one? What's the model seeing that's keeping it from lighting up both of those lanes?

query: Every one of our markets ends up leaning on a single plant in this plan — New York all San Diego, Chicago all Seattle, Topeka all San Diego. That's a lot of single points of failure. Wouldn't it be more resilient to insist every market draw from at least two plants, so no one corridor going down strands a customer? What's the tradeoff that's pushing the model toward sole-sourcing each market instead?

query: Topeka is geographically Seattle's natural turf, yet the plan hands the whole market to San Diego and leaves Seattle out of it. Shouldn't Seattle at least be carrying the larger share into Topeka — its volume on that lane no smaller than what San Diego ships there? What's pulling the optimizer the other way and keeping San Diego as the dominant Topeka supplier?

query: Some of these lanes are basically swallowing a whole market by themselves — San Diego covering all of New York, Seattle covering all of Chicago. That concentration worries me. Shouldn't we cap any single plant-to-market lane so it carries no more than about two-thirds of that market's demand, forcing the rest onto a second supplier? What's the model weighing that keeps it piling each market onto one lane?
